coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial properties of folic acid-based hydrogel loaded with CeCDs and its potential application in food preservation

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengzhen
Zeng, Li
Chen, Hongjun
Hu, Senhao
Huang, Chengzhi
Zhen, Shujun
Zhan, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The development of novel preservation techniques capable of food safety and nutrition retention has received extensive attention. Herein, a biocompatible and biodegradable composite folate/zinc supramolecular hydrogel loaded with Ce-doped carbon dots (named FZCC hydrogel) has been fabricated. This hydrogel was synthesized through hydrogen bonds or metal-ligand coordination and held the advantages of superior antibacterial properties, self-healing properties, and washability. Particularly, CeCDs in the hydrogel possessed photodynamic capabilities under white light irradiation, endowing broad antibacterial ability against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The growth inhibition rate reached up to 83.98 % and 80.30 % for S. aureus and E. coli. Moreover, the hydrogels can release high concentrations of Zn2+ over 15 days, resulting in a sustained antimicrobial effect. The application of FZCC hydrogel to pakchoi cabbages, apples, and cooked meat can effectively prolong the shelf life and ensure food quality by controlling microbial contaminations, providing great potential for designing sustainable active food packaging materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ce-doped carbon dots
Folic acid/zinc supramolecular hydrogel
Synergistic antibacterial strategy
Food preservation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preservation of food by FZCC hydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the CeCDs and FZCC-hydrogel. (a) TEM image of CeCDs. The top inset shows an HRTEM image of CeCDs. The bottom inset shows the size distribution of CeCDs. (b) Photographs of the hydrogels under visible light and UV light of 365 nm. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of hydrogels. (d) SEM images of the freeze-dried hydrogels. (e) FTIR spectra of FA, CeCDs, and hydrogels. (f) UV–vis absorbance spectra of FA, CeCDs, and hydrogels. (g) Frequency sweep measurement for FZCC hydrogel. (h) Time dependence of G' and G" for FZCC hydrogel.

Antibacterial properties of the hydrogels with and without light irradiation. (a) Photographs of the survival of S. aureus and E. coli after incubation with FA and FZCC hydrogel. (b) Quantitative antibacterial efficiency of FA and FZCC hydrogel. (c) The images of FZCC hydrogels over three cycles. (d) The antibacterial rates of the FZCC hydrogel against E. coli and S. aureus for three cycles.

(a) Representative SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli after treatment with different hydrogels, (b) CLSM images of S. aureus and E. coli treated with FZCC hydrogel, respectively. Scale bar: 20 μm. (The fluorescence signals of CeCD originate from their intrinsic fluorescence properties) (c) Quantitative analyses of biofilm by using crystal violet staining. (d, e) Biofilm inhibition activity against S. aureus and E. coli biofilm of FZ and FZCC hydrogel. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Antibacterial mechanism of FZCC hydrogel. (a) The total ROS generation measured by DCFH fluorescence probe. (b, c, d) EPR spectra of O2 −•, OH•, and 1O2 generated by FZCC hydrogel. (e) Proposed mechanism of FZCC hydrogel for bacterial inhibition under light conditions. (g) Confocal fluorescence images of S. aureus and E. coli with different treatments and stained with DHE probe. Scale bar: 20 μm.

Biocompatibility, biodegradability, and washability assessment of FZCC hydrogel. (a) Cell viability treated with FZCC hydrogels; (b) hemolysis rate and photographs of mouse blood; (c) biodegradation of FZCC hydrogel buried in the soil and lake water; (d) fluorescence emission spectra under 360 nm excitation light comparing fluorescence intensity before and after washing and actual images of vegetables.

The total viable counts in foods packaged with FZ and FZCC hydrogel during storage. (a, b) Evaluation of the actual antibacterial application of FZCC hydrogel on vegetables, apples, and cured meat using E. coli as a model. (c, d) Quantitative measurements of long-lasting antibacterial effect of FZCC hydrogel.

General schematic of the fabrication process of FZCC hydrogel for food preservation. (a) Schematic representation of the hydrothermal synthesis procedure of CeCDs. (b) Preparation process of the FZCC hydrogel based on folic acid/zinc supramolecular hydrogel. (c) The application of the FZCC hydrogel in food preservation.

head.ce:title: Antibacterial properties of folic acid-based hydrogel loaded with CeCDs and its potential application in food preservation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of novel preservation techniques capable of food safety and nutrition retention has received extensive attention. Herein, a biocompatible and biodegradable composite folate/zinc supramolecular hydrogel loaded with Ce-doped carbon dots (named FZCC hydrogel) has been fabricated. This hydrogel was synthesized through hydrogen bonds or metal-ligand coordination and held the advantages of superior antibacterial properties, self-healing properties, and washability. Particularly, CeCDs in the hydrogel possessed photodynamic capabilities under white light irradiation, endowing broad antibacterial ability against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The growth inhibition rate reached up to 83.98 % and 80.30 % for S. aureus and E. coli. Moreover, the hydrogels can release high concentrations of Zn2+ over 15 days, resulting in a sustained antimicrobial effect. The application of FZCC hydrogel to pakchoi cabbages, apples, and cooked meat can effectively prolong the shelf life and ensure food quality by controlling microbial contaminations, providing great potential for designing sustainable active food packaging materials.

A biocompatible and biodegradable hydrogel with efficient photodynamic antimicrobial performance based on folic acid, Zn2+, and Ce-doping carbon dots has been successfully developed for food preservation.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage caused by microbial contamination is one of the major reasons for shortening the shelf life of food, resulting in huge food waste and economic losses globally each year (Snyder et al., 2024). To address this challenge, food packaging plays a critical role in maintaining food safety and retarding product deterioration during its storage, transportation, and retail (Kumar et al., 2023). The most common packaging material is synthetic petroleum-based plastic materials, such as polystyrene, polyethylene terephthalate, and polyurethane, are widely used due to their effectiveness in protecting food from oxygen, moisture, and environmental pollutants (Sid et al., 2021). The microplastics and nanoplastics produced create serious environmental and health concerns (Ermis et al., 2024). Therefore, it is imperative to develop eco-friendly, biodegradable, and active packaging systems that possess antimicrobial activity for food preservation.

Hydrogels formed by natural biomolecules, like proteins (Zhang et al., 2023), polysaccharides (Wang et al., 2023), and polyphenols (Li et al., 2022) have been widely applied for fabricating functional packaging films due to their high biocompatibility, biodegradability, and flexible programmability. However, these hydrogels usually show poor mechanical properties and low antibacterial activities, extremely hindering their practical application in food preservation. To overcome these limitations, coordination-triggered crosslinking has been explored as a strategy to engineer hydrogels with enhanced mechanical and functional properties. (Li, Yang, et al., 2017). For example, Liu et al. proposed a facile and robust approach to prepare pintable and injectable hydrogels in aqueous conditions based on coordination between zinc ions (Zn2+) and folate coordination, demonstrating reversible and tunable mechanical properties (Liu et al., 2018). Additionally, to enhance the antibacterial activity of hydrogels, tremendous efforts including the discovery of new bactericidal polymer and the incorporation of antibacterial agent or inorganic nanoparticles have been devoted (Li et al., 2024; Yen et al., 2022). Particularly, light-responsive nanoparticles such as zinc oxide nanoparticles (Alavi & Nokhodchi, 2020), carbon dots (Sun et al., 2024; Wen et al., 2023), porphyrinic metal-organic framework (Zhao et al., 2023), and berberine-3,4,5-methoxycinnamic acid nanoparticles (Xu et al., 2024) have attracted great attention owing to the excellent photodynamic antibacterial performance and then can be used to fabricate antibacterial composite hydrogels/films for preserving food.

Carbon dots, as a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have been regarded as a prospective alternative for photosensitizers in photomedicine due to their outstanding features such as photostability, biocompatibility, low cost, and easy preparation (Chung et al., 2020). To further facilitate the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, Zhang et al. demonstrated that lanthanide ions (Ln3+) could serve as an effective mediator to boost the photo-oxidation capacity of many nanomaterial photosensitizers owing to the long triplet state lifetime of Ln3+ (Zhang et al., 2019). In addition, another attractive strategy is heteroatom/metal-doping in carbon dots to improve the number of active sites (Sekar et al., 2022). Up to now, N-doped (Chen et al., 2023), P-doped (Jiang et al., 2024), and halogen/N co-doped carbon dots (Huang et al., 2021) have been engineered for efficient photodynamic inactivation of bacterial pathogens. Meanwhile, we recently reported that the antibacterial activity of Ce-doped carbon dots (CeCDs) could be enhanced significantly under white light irradiation, offering great promise for photodynamic therapy (Chen et al., 2024).

Encouraged by these findings, we utilized Ce-doping carbon dots (CeCDs) to construct light-active folate/Zn2+ nanocomposite hydrogel (named FZCC hydrogel) for improved food preservation in this work (Scheme 1 ). CeCDs, obtained by one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, cerium citrate, and ethylenediamine, were then fixed into the hydrogel using folic acid as the building block and cross-linking with Zn2+ in situ. Taking advantage of the synergistic action of the photodynamic effects of the embedded CeCDs and the released Zn2+ which also exhibits excellent antibacterial properties, the FZCC hydrogel will achieve rapid sterilization under simulated visible light irradiation and long-lasting antibacterial effects, facilitating the storage and preservation of food products. Given the excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability of FA, Zn2+, and CeCDs, this nanocomposite hydrogel holds great potential in the food packaging industry.

Citric acid (CA), 1, 2-ethylenediamine (EDA), and cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3•6H2O) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Folic acid hydrate and zinc chloride were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). The ROS probes dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and dihydroethidium (DHE) were supplied by Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Propidium iodide (PI) was obtained from Shanghai Bestbio Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (China). The zinc ion assay kit was purchased from Wuhan Elabscience Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Water used in all experiments was purified with a Milli-Q purification system.

The UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded by a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were performed on a JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted by a Hitachi SU8020 field emission scanning electron microscope. Atomic force microscope (AFM) measurement was carried out with AFM-Smart SPM atomic force microscope (HORIBA, France). The XPS measurements were performed an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on an IRPrestige-21 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Japan). Rheological measurements were obtained using a TA rheometer (DHR-2) instrument. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were performed on the Bruker EMX EMXplus-9.5/12 EPR spectrometer at room temperature.

CeCDs were firstly synthesized by a hydrothermal process according to our previous report (Chen et al., 2024). Specifically, CeCDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid, cerium citrate, and ethylenediamine as precursors. For the preparation of FZCC-hydrogel, 0.18 g folic acid was dispersed in sodium hydroxide solution (pH 10.0) with continuous stirring until the solution became clear. Subsequently, CeCDs were incorporated into the above folate solution, followed by the addition of zinc chloride (ZnCl2) solution (0.07 mol/L). The ratio of folic acid to zinc nitrate is 1:1 and the concentration of CeCDs is 3 mg/mL. The mixture was then vortexed and allowed to stand at room temperature for 20 min to obtain a transparent and uniform composite hydrogel (Without CeCDs named FZ hydrogel).

The antimicrobial performance was evaluated using the agar plate counting method (Huang et al., 2020). Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were chosen as the model pathogen. Briefly, bacteria were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C overnight with continuous shaking at 150 rpm to logarithmic growth phase. 100μL of the bacterial suspension (108 CFU/mL) was dropped on the hydrogel surface (The hydrogel volume was 200 μL and the contact surface area was 2.63 cm2) and cultured at 37°C for 40 min with light (3613 lx) or non-light irradiation. Subsequently, the bacterial suspension was diluted 10,000-fold in PBS and 100μL of the resulting solution was spread on LB agar plates. The number of colonies in each sample was calculated after 12h of incubation.

Fresh pakchoi cabbages, apples, and cooked meat were purchased from a local supermarket and then cut into small pieces. They were randomly separated and stored with FZCC hydrogel coating. Bacterial suspension was inoculated onto the surface of the samples and illuminated by white light for 2 h. Groups without hydrogel or white light treatment were designed as controls. The samples were collected and then serially diluted to 104 CFU/mL with PBS. Then 100 μL bacterial cells was plated on LB agar plate, incubated at 37°C for 12 h for subsequent counting of the colonies.

All testes were carried out three times (n = 3) and the data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical significance in this study was evaluated using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA).

TEM and AFM images indicate that the formation of nearly monodisperse CeCDs with an average diameter of ∼4.4nm and a height of ∼1.3 nm (Fig. 1a, Fig. S1a), suggesting that the prepared CeCDs are anisotropic. The corresponding HRTEM result shows characteristic crystal lattice spacing of ∼0.19 nm, which matches well with the (104) crystal planes of graphene (Jiang et al., 2020). Raman spectrum of the CeCDs reveals relatively weak D-band around 1326 cm1 and G-band around 1585 cm−1, and the I D/I G height ratio is 0.96, indicating large portion of defects on their surface (Gao et al., 2023) (Fig. S1b). The functional groups and chemical composition of the CeCDs were further confirmed by FTIR and XPS spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum of the CeCDs displays the typical peaks at 1660 cm−1, 1380 cm−1, 1330 cm−1 which are attributed to CO, C − H, and C − N stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. S1c). The results of XPS full-scale spectra analysis indicate that the CeCDs mainly consist of C (42.67 at.%), N (21.31 at.%), and O (36.02 at.%) (Fig. S1 d-g). The existence of Ce element was verified by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and the concentration of Ce doping in carbon dots was 6.556 μg/L (Fig. S1h). In addition, the XPS results indicate the presence of CC, C − O, CO, and C − N on the surface of CeCDs. These results validate the successful doping of Ce in the CeCDs and abundant -NH2 and -OH functional groups on the surface, making them disperse well in the experimental medium. In the UV–Vis spectrum, two peaks at 240 and 348nm were observed, which are owing to the π–π* transitions of CC and CC bonds in the aromatic sp2 domains and the n–π* transition of the CO and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S1i). The fluorescence emission of CeCDs (excitation wavelength E ex = 362 nm) appears at 452 nm, giving blue fluorescence (Fig. S1i).

Considering that Zn2+ can cross-link folate fibers to form hydrogel, CeCDs may be loaded into the FA hydrogel through hydrogen bond or coordination bonds to form FZCC hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 1b, a brown composite FZCC hydrogel with a smooth surface was successfully obtained. It is highly fluorescent owing to the dispersion of the CeCDs and exhibit fluorescence colors that corresponds to the fluorescence emission wavelengths of the CeCDs (Fig. 1c). SEM images reveal that the FA and FZCC hydrogels have obvious porous structures, and the pore size distributions are approximately 42.04 and 31.56 μm, respectively (Fig. 1d). Notably, the incorporation of CeCDs not only does not disrupt the three-dimensional structure of the FA hydrogels, but also results in higher crosslinking densities and thus smaller pore size. In addition, the interactions of hydrogen bonding and metal coordination in hydrogels were investigated by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1e). Compared to pure FA, the OH stretching vibration peak of the hydrogel exhibits a significant red-shift, indicating the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. (Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2024). Meanwhile, a strong peak located at 1694 cm−1 for the free carbonyl group of solid FA was observed, while this band disappeared in FZ and FZCC hydrogel, suggesting the formation of assembly. In addition, the peak at 418 cm−1 in the hydrogel was due to the metal‑oxygen bond, which was considered a key factor in gelation. Furthermore, the blue-shifts of the COO– asymmetric and symmetric stretching peaks from ca. 1604 to 1608 cm−1 and 1485 to 1508 cm−1 were assigned to the coordinate bond in FA with Zn2+ ions (Wang et al., 2024). Apart from the slight blue-shift CO stretching vibration peak, no new peaks appeared, suggesting that the introduction of CeCDs did not change the structure or chemical properties of the hydrogel. The UV–Vis spectrum of the FZCC hydrogel exhibited characteristic peaks at 276 nm and 452 nm, corresponding to the FZ hydrogel and CeCDs, respectively (Fig. 1f). The decreased absorption intensity could be attributed to the introduction of CeCDs. Rheological analysis was further carried out to test the mechanical properties of the hydrogels by measuring the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G"). The G' values of FZCC hydrogel were always greater than the G" value at gradually enhanced frequencies from 0.1 to 100 rad/s (Fig. 1g), which highlighted the excellent mechanical properties of the FZCC hydrogel. Meanwhile, time sweep experiments showed that FZCC hydrogel possesses good fatigue resistance and structural stability (Fig. 1h). Note that the FZCC hydrogel were still moist and well kept their states even after 8 days of standing at room temperature (Fig. S2). All these indicate the successful formation of composite hydrogel and the structural robustness of the prepared hydrogel. Moreover, hydrogels stained with crystal violet were put in contact with each other. After being left to stand for 5 min, hydrogels coalesced together and could be lifted (Fig. S3).

The antibacterial properties of CeCDs against two different bacterial strains, Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), were evaluated by the plate-counting method firstly (Fig. S4). CeCDs exhibit intrinsic bactericidal activity similar to our previous reports under dark conditions, and LED light exposure alone does not inhibit bacterial growth. (Chen et al., 2024). Obviously, the antibacterial activity increases with increasing CeCDs concentration after light irradiation, suggesting that CeCDs have the dose-dependent bacteria killing capability. The antibacterial efficiency of the CeCDs for S. aureus and E. coli with a concentration of 240 mg/L reaches 99.28 % and 96.42 % within 60 min of exposure, respectively. Motivated by the impressive light-enhanced antibacterial activity of CeCDs, we further evaluated the antibacterial abilities of FZCC hydrogels under light irradiation. The results in Fig. 2a show that beyond dark bactericidal activity, FZ hydrogel also manifest photoinduced effect. Zn2+ is an essential trace element for the survival and proliferation of bacteria, and its cellular concentration can be regulated by Zn2+ transport proteins (Zhao et al., 2024). However, previous studies suggested that excessive accumulation of Zn2+ will destruct the microbial membrane, disrupt enzyme activity, and interfere with amino acid metabolism, inhibiting the bacterial cell growth (Dey et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2022). As expected, Zn2+ exhibited a concentration-dependent antibacterial effect (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, the photocatalytic generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by zinc-based nanoparticles/complexes has been reported and been widely utilized as an antimicrobial material (Du et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2021). In addition, the FA can be photodegraded to yield 6-formylpterin and pterin-6-carboxylic acid, which can also induce ROS generation (Sirelkhatim et al., 2015). Importantly, with the introduction of a small amount of CeCDs, FZCC hydrogel display the strongest antibacterial effect upon light exposure and the antibacterial efficiency increases to 83.98 % and 80.31 % against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Fig. 2b). It can be concluded that benefiting from the synergistic antibacterial effect of CeCDs and FZ hydrogel, FZCC hydrogel can achieve efficient, rapid, and safe antibacterial activity through the photodynamic treatment stimulated by commercial LED light.

Furthermore, the repeated antibacterial experiments of FZCC hydrogel were performed on bacteria S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. S6). The FZCC hydrogel previously used for antibacterial activity were sterilized by ultraviolet radiation. Results in Fig. 2c&d showed that the antibacterial rates exceed 90 % after three cycles and ultraviolet irradiation does not destroy the structure of FZCC. The reusability of FZCC hydrogel makes it a promising economical and convenient material in food packaging. The antibacterial results against Salmonella and Bacillus subtilis further validate the broad-spectrum antibacterial performance of the FZCC hydrogel (Fig. S7). The FZCC hydrogel maintained strong antibacterial efficacy after 3 months at both room temperature and 4 °C, demonstrating excellent chemical and physical stability, which is essential for long-term food packaging applications (Fig. S8).

To understand the antibacterial mechanism of FZCC hydrogel, the surface potential (represented as Zeta potential) of bacteria was first analyzed (Fig. S9). The bacterial cells were negative at neutral pH, while slight increases in ζ values were observed after incubation with FZCC hydrogel, pointing out that FZCC attached to bacteria and altered their surface properties. The morphology changes and membrane integrity of E. coli and S. aureus were further examined by SEM. As shown in Fig. 3a, numerous bacterial cells reveal smooth and intact cell morphology without treatment. In contrast, the bacteria on the FZCC hydrogel exhibit obvious shrunken and engulfed morphologies and some intracellular matter leaks off from the bacteria, whereas the morphologies of bacteria treated with the FZ hydrogel are comparable to those control bacteria with slightly wrinkled and ruptured. Moreover, the effect of FZCC hydrogel on E. coli genome was investigated based on agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. S10). Obvious DNA damage occurred with prolonged incubation time.

The release behavior of CeCDs and Zn2+ from FZCC hydrogel was further assessed using the calibration curve (Fig. S11). Firstly, the accumulative concentration of CeCDs continuously increases over time and about 0.35 mg/mL of CeCDs were released within 120 min under physiological condition. When the hydrogel was immersed in solutions at pH 5 and 7.4 buffer (Fig. S12), the release rate of CeCDs increased owing to the rupture of hydrogen bonds. The growth of bacteria was known to produce acidic environments (Riduan & Zhang, 2021), which could accelerate the release of CeCDs. In addition, it should be noted that the Zn2+ release from FZCC hydrogel exhibited a sustained release behavior, where the cumulative Zn2+ concentration was significantly increased to 0.01 mg/mL at the first days and then maintain 0.03 mg/mL. The long-term release of Zn2+ has been confirmed to play important role in the bacteria-killing activity (Juzeniene et al., 2016). Moreover, confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was employed to evaluate the internalization of CeCDs based on the innate intense fluorescence of CeCDs. Fig. 3b illustrated obvious green fluorescence inside both S. aureus and E. coli after co-cultivation for 60 min, indicating that CeCDs can effectively intercalate into bacterial membranes and enter the cytoplasm.

In addition, the antibiofilm activity was further investigated by crystal violet (CV) staining assay. As shown in Fig. 3c-e, S. aureus biofilm formation was significantly inhibited after incubation with hydrogels. Specifically, the FZCC hydrogel possessed high biofilm removal efficacy compared with the FZ group under light irradiation. A similar result was found with E. coli. The results supported that incorporating CeCDs can greatly eliminate bacterial biofilms.

The generation of ROS was detected by a commonly used ROS probe dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) (Fig. 4a). Under white light irradiation, DCFH alone is nearly non-fluorescent. However, remarkable fluorescence intensity enhancement is observed in the presence of FZCC composite hydrogel, demonstrating DCFH is oxidized by ROS that produced from the hydrogel. In comparison, CeCDs have higher ROS generation efficiency than the FZ hydrogel. It is worth noting that there was no ROS generation when the cells were incubated with hydrogels but without light irradiation (Fig. S13). To identify the types of ROS, EPR spectroscopy was additionally employed using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin-trap agent for highly reactive 1O2 (Fig. 4b-d). A typical paramagnetic absorption is observed and matches the 1O2 signal. Thereafter, the generation of superoxide radical O2 −• and hydroxyl radical OH• are verified by spin trapper 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide (DMPO). These results suggest that FACC hydrogel can produce O2 − •, OH•, and 1O2 in aqueous solution upon light irradiation. Furthermore, dihydroethidium (DHE) indicator was used as a fluorescent probe to evaluate the intracellular ROS generation ability (Fig. 4e). The bright red fluorescence of DHE in bacterial cells was observed after incubation with FZCC hydrogel, indicating the high-level generation of ROS. According to the above observation, we can conclude that the antibacterial mechanism of FZCC hydrogel involves the release, internalization, and rupture processes (Fig. S14). Specifically, the release of CeCDs and Zn2+ can attach and pass through the bacterial wall to damage the cell membrane and disrupt bacterial metabolism. The ROS generation under light irradiation cause oxidative stress, responsible for killing of bacterial cells and biofilm elimination.

Biocompatibility is a fundamental requirement for food preservation, and we firstly performed a CCK-8 method to evaluate the cytotoxicity of FZCC or the extracts of FZCC (Fig. 5a). Compared with the control, FZCC hydrogel displayed no obvious effect on the cell viability after treatment for 24 h. Notably, cell viability was greater than 95 % even with light treatment. In the hemolysis assay, the hemolysis ratios of the FZCC hydrogel were all within the acceptable range (<5 %) according to the Standard Practice for Assessment of Hemolytic Properties of Materials (Fig. 5b). The above results indicate that the FZCC hydrogel possesses low acute toxicity and excellent biocompatibility and may be safe as food packaging for further applications.

The biodegradation of the FZCC hydrogel was further assessed. The FZCC was broken and gradually disappeared after 14 d, indicating that FZCC can be fully degraded in liquid environment and soil environment (Fig. 5c), which was attributed to the breakdown of hydrogen bond and the complexation of zinc ions, hence avoiding the contamination of water and the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preservation of food by FZCC hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applications of FZCC hydrogel were assessed in pakchoi cabbages, apples, and cooked meat preservation. The washability of the FZCC hydrogel were investigated firstly. Vegetables with hydrogel layer were immersed in tap er water. The results in Fig. 5d show that almost no fluorescence signal can be collected after washing, confirming that the FZCC hydrogel can be easily removed by rinsing with water. Even if the FZCC hydrogel was applied to the apples for 3 days, it can still be completely washed off (Fig. S15). In addition, the growth of E. coli was significantly inhibited with FZCC hydrogel coating, the colonies of E. coli being much lower than those for the samples without any protection (Fig. 6 a&b). Besides, the FZCC hydrogel showed a sustained inhibition advantage over the FZ during the storage period (Fig. 6 c&d). This phenomenon may be attributed to the sustainable release of CeCDs from the hydrogel matrix, maintaining the concentration of CeCDs on the contact surface sufficiently enough to suppress E. coli growth. Due to its excellent antibacterial performance, self-healing properties, and biological safety, the strategy of coating a layer of FZCC hydrogel on the surface of food can effectively inhibit the growth of pathogenic bacteria on the food surface, thereby extending the shelf life of food. Sensory evaluations revealed that FZCC hydrogel-treated food exhibited enhanced flavor, color, and texture compared to controls, confirming its non-detrimental effect on food quality (Fig. S16).

In summary, CeCDs with inherent antibacterial property were successfully incorporated into a folate/zinc supramolecular hydrogel (FZCC). Following light irradiation, the designed FZCC hydrogel display significantly enhanced antibacterial activity against multiple food-contaminating bacteria by rapidly generating ROS. Meanwhile, the release of CeCDs and Zn2+ endowed the hydrogel with long-lasting and renewable antibacterial properties. More importantly, based on the use of safe and natural raw materials, the hydrogel possesses desirable biocompatibility, making it suitable for the coating of many foods such as pakchoi cabbages, apples, and cooked meat to prevent microbial spoilage. The washability and biodegradability of FZCC hydrogel avoid food safety concerns and minimize the environmental burden. Overall, the FZCC hydrogel provides great potential as an effective and convenient polymeric material for food preservation.

Mengzhen Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Li Zeng: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongjun Chen: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Senhao Hu: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chengzhi Huang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Shujun Zhen: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology. Lei Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 22134005), Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179), and Chongqing Municipal Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (S202410635026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.144031.
